subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: The exploration is developed in a clear, logical sequence with coherent sections linked from definitions through heuristics to conclusion.
Headings and sub-headings delineate introduction, body and conclusion effectively, aiding navigation.
Figures are generally numbered and integrated to support the text, contributing to organization.
weaknesses: Noticeable repetition (duplicated conclusion, repeated Big-O explanations) reduces conciseness.
Occasional digressions (extended video summaries) interrupt the flow of argument.
Several figures are misnumbered or have misplaced captions, and bibliography entries are duplicated or inconsistently formatted.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates sound coherence and logical development (Band 3) and is well organized with clear structure (Band 3). However, repetition and minor organizational lapses (misplaced captions, duplicate bibliography entries) prevent it reaching the highest band for conciseness (Band 2). A score of 3/4 reflects good performance overall with room for improvement in conciseness and editorial polish.

strengths: Standard graph-theoretic terms (vertex, edge, degree) are used correctly, and algebraic expressions appear consistently.
Multiple representations (textual explanation, tables, diagrams of graphs and routes) are employed effectively to illustrate concepts.
The overall communication shows a mostly clear logical structure with topic sentences guiding each section.
weaknesses: Mathematical notation errors (e.g. â1Â·10Â° 13â mis-formatted) and typos reduce clarity.
Some sections lack smooth transitions (sudden shifts from one-tree to greedy algorithm) affecting coherence.
Explanations of formulae are sometimes brief or ambiguous without sufficient contextual interpretation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student uses a range of representations effectively (Band 3) and mostly consistent mathematical language, but occasional notation errors and thin logical links keep it below the top band. A mark of 3/4 acknowledges strong use of multiple forms and general clarity, offset by the need for tighter notation and commentary.

strengths: A personal voice emerges occasionally, indicating some engagement with the chosen topic.
Selection of a contemporary problem (TSP algorithms) shows initial personal interest.
weaknesses: Reliance on external sources (e.g. Wikipedia) without paraphrase limits originality.
No novel data sets, independent coding or distinctive modelling choices are presented.
Limited evidence of independent thinking beyond compiling known algorithms.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All strands sit firmly in the lowest non-zero band (Band 1). The work shows some personal voice and initiative but lacks substantial original contribution or deep independent thinking. Therefore, 1/3 is appropriate.

strengths: Includes brief qualitative comments on what was learned and some trade-offs between time and optimality.
weaknesses: Reflection is descriptive and generic, lacking quantitative comparisons or linkages to specific results.
Critical analysis of outcomes and future implications is minimal and superficial.
No detailed evaluation of algorithm performance on actual datasets.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Reflection is present but remains at a basic level across depth, critical analysis and evaluation (Band 1). The comments are general, with no substantial insight or data-driven evaluation, warranting 1/3.

strengths: Relevant mathematics (factorials, Big-O notation, MST definitions, probability formulae) is used appropriately throughout.
Mathematics aligns well with SL course level and is commensurate with syllabus expectations.
Shows generally correct understanding of core concepts, with mostly accurate workings.
weaknesses: Formatting errors (mis-typed exponents) and omitted derivations (e.g. halving factor for mirror tours) introduce minor inaccuracies.
Conceptual slips (misstatement on Eulerian tours) and lack of implementation detail reduce rigor.
No parameter-tuning discussion or worked numerical examples to deepen mathematical analysis.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration applies relevant mathematics appropriately and shows good SL-level understanding with only minor errors (Band 3). While deeper proofs and numerical work are absent, the use of complexity and graph theory is solid. A score of 3/6 reflects competent mathematical application with scope for greater depth and precision.

strandSelections.reasoning: Paragraphs generally begin with a topic sentence followed by clarification, but logical links inside some sections are thin (e.g. sudden jump from one-tree to greedy algorithm). Minor notation errors also affect clarity, placing the work in the âsome logical structure and clarityâ band.

The student shows correct understanding of factorial growth and greedy conditions, yet mis-formats numbers and omits justification of the 1.1 ratio.  A few conceptual slips ("Eulerian graph doesn't allows for revisiting vertices") indicate partial understanding with errors; hence band 2.

The student employs text, algebraic expressions, tables, and many diagrams of graphs/routes.  Although most graphics are imported, they are integrated to support explanations, satisfying âeffective use of different formsâ.

The exploration proceeds in a generally logical sequence (definition â complexity â exact method â heuristics â meta-heuristics â conclusion).  Ideas are connected and there are no disjoint sections, so the reader can follow the argument.  Nevertheless, sections sometimes feel like summaries of external videos rather than an argument built step-by-step, and occasional digressions (e.g. long YouTube examples) weaken the flow, preventing the exemplary level.

Selecting a contemporary topic shows some initiative, yet no novel data set, original code, or distinctive modelling choice is presented. Creativity is limited to compiling material, so the work fits the lowest non-zero band.

The student offers brief qualitative comments about trade-offs between time and optimality but no quantitative evaluation (e.g. comparison of heuristic lengths).  This constitutes only basic analysis.

There is a cursory statement that Christofides is â1.1Ãâ the lower bound and âvery impressiveâ, yet no computation or discussion of implications for specific data sets.  Evaluation is therefore limited.
Reflection is confined to a general concluding section describing what the student âhas learnedâ.  There is no ongoing commentary linking results to objectives, so reflection remains basic.

Standard terms such as âvertexâ, âedgeâ, âdegreeâ, âO(n!)â are used correctly, but mathematical symbols are occasionally mis-typed (e.g. â1Â·10Â° 13â, ââ 1Â·10Â° 13 toursâ).  Explanations of formulae are brief and sometimes ambiguous.  The usage is therefore âsome appropriateâ, not yet mostly consistent.

A personal voice appears occasionally (âWith this IA I have deepened my understandingâ¦â), but the approach follows textbook structure and externally sourced images.  Hence only âsome evidence of personal approachâ is visible.

Most of the writing is on-task, but there is noticeable repetition (two almost identical concluding paragraphs, repeated explanations of Big-O, long quotations).  Several images add little beyond what is stated in the text.  Hence the work is âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elementsâ rather than fully concise.

Factorials, Big-O, MST definition, and probability formulae are relevant but applied only illustratively.  No algorithm is run on real coordinates, and few numerical results are produced, so relevance is present but application limited.

The investigation largely recounts known algorithms; original mathematical thinking (e.g. deriving a bound, coding a heuristic, testing data) is absent.  The factorial tour-count example is routine.  There is therefore only limited evidence of independent thinking.

Headings and sub-headings create a clear introduction, body and conclusion.  Figures are numbered (though some captions are misplaced), and the reader can locate content quickly.  Minor weaknessesâmissing page numbers, repeated bibliography entriesâstop it just short of âexceptionally well organisedâ.

The mathematics (graph theory notions, complexity, exponential expressions) lies within HL syllabus but is not developed to HL depth (no proofs, no Dijkstra/linear programming, etc.).  Most content matches SL/HL core; thus it is âmostly at course levelâ.

annotations.comment: Figure 9âs image is misnumbered and lacks a caption; ensure consistent numbering and descriptive caption matching text references.
The statement that an Eulerian graph âdoesn't allow revisiting verticesâ is incorrect; clarify that Eulerian tours traverse each edge once but revisit vertices.
The Eulerian tour sequence is shown but not explained; interpret why vertices repeat and how the shortcutting produces a valid TSP tour.
The student relies heavily on a Wikipedia definition without paraphrasing or adding their own interpretation, limiting personal engagement and originality.
Caption for Fig 1 is placed far from its reference in the text and lacks integration; reposition and explicitly discuss the figureâs relevance.
The Big O explanation is generic; include a concrete TSP example (e.g. 17 cities) to show how each complexity class applies.
The Big-O chart is generic and not explicitly tied to TSP growth; clarify how the axes correspond to number of cities and operations.
The derivation of the halving factor for symmetric tours is asserted but not justified; explain why dividing by 2 eliminates mirror tours.
Notation â1Â·10Â° 13â is unclear and mis-formatted; use proper exponent syntax such as $$1\times10^{13}$$ consistently.
The heuristic definition is too general; illustrate with a simple TSP example to show how trial-and-error steps proceed.
The NN heuristic complexity is stated as $O(n^2)$ but the source citation is ambiguous; provide a clearer reference and brief derivation.
The approximation-ratio formula is given without a worked example; calculate the ratio for a small TSP instance to demonstrate its use.
The claim that removing one edge from a TSP tour yields an MST is oversimplified; discuss conditions under which the resulting tree is actually minimal.
Primâs algorithm description is clear but a pseudocode listing or stepwise outline would strengthen mathematical rigour.
The Primâs algorithm figure lacks guided commentary; describe each highlighted step to improve clarity.
One-tree concept is introduced without a formal definition or labeled diagram; add precise definitions and reference elements in the figure.
The greedy-method description ends mid-sentence and leaves the approach incomplete; ensure text is fully included and coherent.
A quantitative comparison of greedy performance (1.17 vs 1.25) is cited without data or calculations; include actual tour weights.
Fig 7 is not interpreted in the text; discuss what the example demonstrates and how it supports the greedy explanation.
The description of perfect matching cites polynomial methods but omits naming or summarizing them; include at least one matching algorithm.
The summary lacks any evaluation of Christofidesâ 1.1Ã ratio impact on specific datasets; reflect on actual performance implications.
The algorithm steps are listed clearly but lack commentary on computational complexity or implementation considerations for each step.
A pseudocode outline and complexity analysis for 2-opt would deepen mathematical rigour, rather than a text-only description.
The discussion of k-opt issues is conceptual; include a small example showing how a local minimum traps 2-opt to illustrate the point.
The acceptance probability formula $$e^{-\Delta/T}$$ is given without discussing how to choose or schedule T; elaborate on parameter tuning.
The antâcolony probability formula lacks mention of pheromone evaporation or decay; discuss how pheromone levels are regulated over iterations.
Rewardâupdate formula is shown without explaining edge normalization or limits on pheromone saturation; add clarity on practical implementation.
The conclusion section is nearly duplicated on consecutive pages, indicating unnecessary repetition; consolidate to improve conciseness.
Reflective comments are generic and describe learning broadly; link insights to specific methodsâ strengths, weaknesses, or results obtained.
The bibliography contains duplicate entries and inconsistent formatting of titles and dates; apply a uniform citation style and remove repeats.
The final references lack access dates and author details for online sources, especially Wikipedia; include full citation details consistently.
